notes: BG 2.22 is the trishtubh-meter body-as-clothing simile (vāsāṁsi jīrṇāni / yathā vihāya / navāni gṛhṇāti naro 'parāṇi). The engineering layer preserves the simile's exact structure: as a person sheds worn-out clothing and acquires a fresh wardrobe, so the embodied requirement sheds worn-out implementations and inhabits successive replacements. The dehī (embodied one) is mapped to the customer-requirement; the śarīra (body) to implementation (language, framework, deployment shape). Both halves of the simile are preserved — the wearer is unchanging, the cloth changes. Shankara's continuity-of-substrate reading (the dehī is to bodies as the man is to clothes) is honoured by the explicit framing 'the requirement walked through every wardrobe-transition essentially undamaged.' Ramanuja's affect-reframing reading (clothes-change is normally a happy event; body-change should be the same) is honoured by the junior-vs-senior framing. No mistranslation. PASS.
